schools.summary: Paul states 'this is the third time I am coming to you' — his third intended visit — and cites the legal rule (Deuteronomy 19:15): 'in the mouth of two or three witnesses shall every word be established'; applying the Mosaic principle to church discipline, he will proceed against offenders not rashly but by due process, on the testimony of two or three witnesses — every charge duly established, his discipline orderly and just (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Chrysostom, Henry): by citing this rule, Paul signals that on his coming he will hold a formal inquiry and act judicially against the impenitent — not arbitrarily, but with the justice the law requires.

themes: This is the third time I am coming to you
In the mouth of two or three witnesses shall every word be established
the third coming and the rule of witnesses
just and orderly discipline

schools.summary: Paul 'told them before' (on his second visit) and now 'foretells (forewarns)' again, 'as if present' though absent, addressing 'them which heretofore have sinned, and to all other' — giving plain warning to the guilty; the solemn declaration 'if I come again, I will not spare' — he will no longer be lenient with the impenitent, but will exercise his apostolic authority in discipline; his forbearance has a limit (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Calvin): Paul gives this forewarning precisely so that they may repent and he may not have to act; the threat is meant to bring them to amendment before he comes.

themes: I told you before, and foretell you... that, if I come again, I will not spare
Paul's solemn warning that he will not spare the impenitent
the limit of forbearance
the warning meant to bring repentance

schools.summary: Paul takes up the Corinthians' own demand: 'since ye seek a proof of Christ speaking in me' — they wanted evidence that Christ truly spoke and acted through him — he will give it, in his coming discipline; for Christ, speaking in Paul, is 'not weak toward you, but mighty in you' — far from the weakness his critics alleged, Christ's power had been displayed among them (in their conversion, the miracles, the gifts) and would be displayed again in discipline (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: if they demand proof of Christ's power in Paul, they will find it — but in a way they may not wish, in the exercise of judgment against the impenitent.

themes: ye seek a proof of Christ speaking in me, which to you-ward is not weak, but is mighty in you
the proof of Christ speaking in Paul
Christ's power not weak but mighty
proof to be shown in discipline

schools.summary: Paul grounds his argument in Christ: Christ 'was crucified through (in) weakness' — submitting to the apparent weakness of the cross — 'yet he liveth by the power of God,' raised and reigning in divine power; and Paul applies it to himself — like his Master, he too is 'weak in him' (sharing Christ's weakness, appearing lowly and suffering) 'but shall live with him by the power of God toward you,' manifesting Christ's power in dealing with them (Barnes, Robertson).

Wesley (with Calvin): as Christ's weakness was followed by resurrection power, so Paul's apparent weakness will be followed by a powerful exercise of authority among the Corinthians; the servant follows the Master's pattern.

themes: though he was crucified through weakness, yet he liveth by the power of God
we also are weak in him, but we shall live with him by the power of God toward you
the pattern of Christ crucified in weakness yet living by God's power
the servant following the Master

schools.summary: Paul turns the demand for proof back on them: instead of testing him, 'examine yourselves, whether ye be in the faith; prove your own selves' — test and prove your own genuineness as believers; the criterion being that they should know 'Jesus Christ is in you' (by his Spirit, by faith, by the new life), the indwelling Christ being the proof of true Christianity; 'except ye be reprobates (adokimoi, disapproved, counterfeit, those that fail the test)' — unless false professors, Christ dwells in them (Barnes, Robertson, Wesley).
Calvin: the true proof of faith is the indwelling Christ, known by his Spirit and the new life; believers are to examine themselves by this mark, and only the reprobate (counterfeit) lack it.

themes: Examine yourselves, whether ye be in the faith; prove your own selves
how that Jesus Christ is in you, except ye be reprobates
the call to self-examination
the indwelling Christ the proof of faith

schools.summary: Paul expresses his 'trust (hope, confidence)' that the Corinthians 'shall know that we are not reprobates (disapproved, counterfeit)' — that he will not fail the test, but will be proved a true and approved apostle; the link to v.5 being that if Christ is in them (as their conversion proves), then Paul, through whom they were converted, is no reprobate but a true minister; their genuineness vindicates his (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Calvin): Paul hopes they will recognize his approval not by his exercising severe discipline but by the evident fruit of his ministry among them.

themes: I trust that ye shall know that we are not reprobates
Paul's trust that he is not a reprobate
their conversion vindicating his apostleship
recognized by his fruit

schools.summary: Paul 'prays to God that ye do no evil' — his desire is for their holiness — 'not that we should appear approved' (not to vindicate his own reputation by their obedience) 'but that ye should do that which is honest (good, honourable)'; his remarkable selflessness being that he would rather they do right even if it meant he 'be as a reprobate' (appear without proof of his power, having no occasion to display authority in discipline) — their good matters more to him than his own vindication (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Henry): a true minister cares more for his people's holiness than for his own credit; Paul would forgo the very proof of his authority if only they would do well.

themes: I pray to God that ye do no evil; not that we should appear approved
that ye should do that which is honest, though we be as reprobates
Paul's prayer for their goodness, not his own reputation
valuing their holiness above his credit

schools.summary: Paul's maxim: 'we can do nothing against the truth, but for the truth' — his apostolic power is given to support and advance the truth, not to oppose it; he can act only in its service; the link to v.7 being that if the Corinthians do well (live in the truth), Paul has no occasion and indeed no power to act against them in discipline, his authority operating only on behalf of the truth, against error and sin, never against righteousness (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the minister's power is entirely subordinate to the truth; it can never be used to crush the obedient, but only to defend the truth and correct what opposes it.

themes: we can do nothing against the truth, but for the truth
Paul's power serves the truth, not against it
authority subordinate to the truth
the obedient safe from his power

schools.summary: Paul 'is glad, when we are weak, and ye are strong' — he rejoices if he appears weak (has no occasion to use stern authority) provided the Corinthians 'are strong' (in faith and holiness); their strength is his joy, even at the cost of his own seeming weakness; and his earnest wish is 'your perfection (katartisis, complete restoration, full setting-right, maturity)' — that they may be thoroughly mended, restored to soundness and complete in every grace (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Calvin): Paul's whole desire is their spiritual completeness; he would gladly be thought weak if only they were perfected in Christ.

themes: we are glad, when we are weak, and ye are strong
this also we wish, even your perfection
gladness in weakness if they are strong
the wish for their perfection

schools.summary: Paul explains why he writes this severe warning 'being absent': it is 'lest being present I should use sharpness (severity)' — he writes ahead so that they may amend, and he need not deal harshly when he comes; the power the Lord gave him being 'to edification (building up), and not to destruction (tearing down)' — even his sharpness, if used, would aim to save and build, not to ruin (Barnes, Robertson).
Chrysostom (with Calvin): Paul prefers to warn by letter and win their repentance, rather than wound by severe discipline in person; his authority is reluctant to strike.

themes: I write these things being absent, lest being present I should use sharpness
the power which the Lord hath given me to edification, and not to destruction
writing beforehand to avoid sharpness
authority for edification

schools.summary: Paul's affectionate close: 'farewell (chairete — rejoice, or farewell). Be perfect (katartizesthe — be perfected, mended, restored); be of good comfort (be comforted, exhorted); be of one mind (think the same, agree); live in peace' — a string of brief, earnest charges, with special stress, to a faction-torn church, on harmony and the healing of their divisions; and the promise that 'the God of love and peace shall be with you' — his presence following where love and peace are pursued (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Henry): if they live in love and peace, the God who is the author and giver of both will be with them; unity and peace are the chief needs of this divided church, and God's presence is the promised reward.

themes: Be perfect, be of good comfort, be of one mind, live in peace
the God of love and peace shall be with you
the closing exhortation
the promise of the God of love and peace

schools.summary: Paul's familiar parting charge: 'greet one another with an holy kiss' — the customary token of Christian love and fellowship in the early church (as in Romans 16:16; 1 Corinthians 16:20), a kiss of peace; the kiss 'holy' (hagios — pure, chaste, sacred), expressing genuine brotherly affection and unity among believers, distinguished from any worldly or impure greeting; coming after the call to be of one mind (v.11), it was the visible sign of the reconciliation and mutual love Paul urged on the divided Corinthians (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: Greet one another with an holy kiss
the charge to greet with a holy kiss
the token of Christian fellowship
the sign of reconciliation

schools.summary: Paul conveys the greeting of 'all the saints' — all the believers where he was (probably Macedonia) — who 'salute (greet) you'; a token of the universal fellowship of the church; not a few individuals but 'all the saints' join in the greeting, the whole company of believers sending their love to the Corinthians, expressing the bond uniting the churches and reminding the divided and troubled Corinthians that they belong to the wider family of God (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: All the saints salute you
the greeting of all the saints
the universal fellowship of the church
the wider family of God

schools.summary: This famous benediction names the three Persons of the Godhead — 'the Lord Jesus Christ,' 'God' (the Father), and 'the Holy Ghost' — and invokes a distinct blessing from each: 'the grace (free favour) of the Lord Jesus Christ' (our access to the Father through Christ's grace), 'the love of God' (the Father's love, the fountain of all), and 'the communion (fellowship, koinonia) of the Holy Ghost' (our partaking of the Spirit and his fellowship); one of the clearest scriptural witnesses to the Trinity and a model of Christian blessing (Barnes, Robertson).

Cyril (with Calvin): the equal invocation of the three Persons together attests their distinct personhood and unity of Godhead; the benediction is a standing proof of the Trinity, the grace of Christ leading to the love of the Father and sealed in the communion of the Spirit.

themes: The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, and the love of God, and the communion of the Holy Ghost, be with you all
the trinitarian benediction
the three Persons invoked in one blessing
a witness to the Trinity
